taskDescription: Click on the key radiology and imaging terms in the text below.
textField:
The five *radiographic* *densities* from most to least opaque are *metal*, *bone*, soft tissue, *fat*, and *air*. On a standard X-ray, dense structures appear white while air appears black. Free gas under the *diaphragm* on an erect abdominal X-ray is called *pneumoperitoneum* and indicates *perforation* of a hollow *viscus*, constituting a surgical emergency.

*Barium* sulfate is the standard *contrast* agent for GI studies but is *contraindicated* when perforation is suspected. In such cases, water-soluble *iodinated* contrast like *Gastrografin* is used instead. CT scanning uses *Hounsfield* units to measure tissue *attenuation*. Water is defined as zero HU, *cortical* bone measures +400 to +1000 HU, and air measures -1000 HU.

*Ultrasound* is the first-line investigation for *biliary* disease and pregnancy as it uses no *ionising* radiation. *MRI* provides superior soft tissue contrast using *magnetic* fields and is preferred for brain, spine, and joint imaging. *Fluoroscopy* provides real-time imaging and is used for *barium* swallow and meal studies.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review radiology terminology and imaging modalities.
Good effort! Revisit the terms you missed.
Excellent! You identified the key radiology terms accurately.
